extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r1, whose condition is (hypertension AND prior_gi_bleed AND seizure_history AND anticoagulated). rec_drug_b is set true only by r4, whose condition is (hypertension). Crucially, r1's condition includes hypertension as a conjunct, so any input that fires r1 necessarily also satisfies r4's condition. Because r1 and r4 target different outputs (rec_drug_a vs rec_drug_b), priority-based conflict resolution never pits them against each other; both fire independently. No rule and no default sets rec_drug_a or rec_drug_b back to false once a rule has set it. Therefore the assignment hypertension=true, prior_gi_bleed=true, seizure_history=true, anticoagulated=true makes both rec_drug_a (via r1) and rec_drug_b (via r4) true simultaneously, violating the mutual-exclusion property. heart_failure and peptic_ulcer are irrelevant (set false here); they only affect rec_drug_c via r2/r3.
